title: Star Wars: Dark Empire
content:
{{Top|rwm|new|italics=1}} {{Youmay|the comic book arc detailing Palpatine's resurrection|the [[Dark Empire|titular portion]] of the [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Galactic Empire]] run by the reborn Palpatine|linebreak=1}} {{Citation}} {{ComicSeries |image=[[File:Darkempiretpb.jpg]] |title=''Star Wars: Dark Empire'' |writer=[[Tom Veitch]] |penciller=[[Cam Kennedy]] |inker=Cam Kennedy |letterer=[[Todd Klein]] |colorist=Cam Kennedy |cover artist=[[Dave Dorman]] |editor= |publisher=[[Dark Horse Comics]] |start date=[[December 12]], [[1991]]{{DarkHorse|url=Comics/91-001/Star-Wars-Dark-Empire-1-of-6|text=Star Wars: Dark Empire #1 (of 6)}} |end date=[[October 20]], [[1992]]{{Marvel|url=comics/issue/54062/star_wars_dark_empire_1991_6|text=Star Wars: Dark Empire (1991) #6}} |schedule=One issue every two months |format=Completed |issues=6 |timeline=[[10 ABY/Legends|10 ABY]]''[[Star Wars: The Comics Companion]]'' |series= |preceded by= |followed by= }} {{Quote|My father's destiny is my own.|Luke Skywalker|[[Dark Empire 2|''Dark Empire'' 2]]}} '''''Star Wars: Dark Empire''''' was a series of [[Star Wars comic books|comic books]] written by [[Tom Veitch]] and illustrated by [[Cam Kennedy]]. They were originally released by [[Dark Horse Comics]] between December 1991 and October 1992 as a 6-issue bi-monthly series, which were later compiled and sold as trade paperbacks and hardcovers. The name generally applies to both the original ''Dark Empire'', as well as its two sequels ''[[Star Wars: Dark Empire II]]'' and ''[[Star Wars: Empire's End]]''. All three comic series detailed the resurrection of [[Galactic Emperor/Legends|Emperor]] [[Palpatine/Legends|Palpatine]] in [[Cloning/Legends|clone]] bodies. ==Publisher's summary== [[File:DETPB-FullCoverArt.jpg|thumb|left|300px|Luke Skywalker in his father's armor with World Devastators and Star Destroyers in the background]] ''Six years after the Battle of Endor, the fight for freedom rages on. Darth Vader is dead, but a reborn Empire—under a mysterious new leader—strikes back at the struggling Rebel Alliance. Massive World Devastators, more powerful and unstoppable than the Death Star, ravage entire planets, while the ruthless heirs of Jabba the Hutt place a monumental price on the heads of Princess Leia and her husband, Han Solo. Along with Lando, Chewbacca, Artoo, Threepio, and other old allies, Han and Leia struggle to protect the future for their unborn child. But their greatest foe may be their closest friend: Luke Skywalker…'' ==Opening crawl== {{OpeningCrawl |name=APPROXIMATELY TEN YEARS
AFTER THE BATTLE OF YAVIN... |text=Following the deaths of Darth Vader and the Emperor at the Battle of Endor, the Rebel Alliance formed a New Republic over much of the galaxy. Long years of struggle have ensued. Remnants of the Empire have regained strength and reclaimed the majority of worlds, including the city-world of Coruscant. Now a civil war within the resurgent Empire has erupted, and the New Republic has seized the opportunity to increase confusion. A recent raid over Coruscant has left Luke Skywalker and Lando Calrissian stranded on the war-torn planet, but help is on the way...}} ==Plot summary== {{Quote|And now, my young apprentice… Your father is dead. Have you come to join me? Will you take the place that rightfully belongs to you… at my side?|Palpatine|}} During the [[Imperial Civil War|Imperial Mutiny]], the [[Alliance to Restore the Republic/Legends|Rebel Alliance]] sought to weaken the factions as much as possible by surreptitiously entering systems and battles with captured [[Star Destroyer/Legends|Star Destroyers]] broadcasting falsified signals, and provoking attacks by one group against another. On one of these raids to the [[Coruscant system/Legends|Coruscant system]], in the Star Destroyer [[Liberator (Imperial I-class)|''Liberator'']] (formerly the ''Adjudicator''), an [[Imperial-class Star Destroyer/Legends|''Imperial''-class Star Destroyer]] captured at the [[Battle of Endor/Legends|Battle of Endor]] and, over a five-year period, repaired and modified for covert missions, and its sister ship, the ''[[Emancipator]]'' (originally the ''Accuser''), entered the [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]] system, where three Star Destroyers demanded novel and unusual codes for clearance. The codes the Alliance had stolen or bartered with slicers for did not work, and [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] and [[Lando Calrissian/Legends|Lando Calrissian]] were shot down over the former [[Galactic City/Legends|Imperial City]]. They should have perished, but successfully landed the gargantuan vessel without many casualties on the surface of Coruscant. For the next several days, Luke, Lando, and their forces hid and fought Imperial battalions. [[File:BattleDebris.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Arrival at Coruscant]] When the news reached [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]] and [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia Organa]], they rushed to the rescue along with [[Chewbacca/Legends|Chewbacca]] and [[C-3PO/Legends|C-3PO]]. They arrived in the nick of time to rescue Lando and the crew of the ''Liberator''. However, Luke informed them of a great disturbance in [[The Force/Legends|the Force]]. At that moment, a [[Force storm (wormhole)|Force storm]] came out of a [[Hyperspace/Legends|hyperspace]] wormhole. Luke told everyone to go, but [[R2-D2/Legends|R2-D2]] stayed with him. As the [[Millennium Falcon/Legends|''Falcon'']] took off, Luke and R2-D2 were sucked in by the anomaly. Later, at the Alliance base on the fifth moon of [[Da Soocha]], code-named [[Pinnacle Base]], the Alliance leaders reported that the separate Imperial factions had disappeared into the unknown regions of the [[Core Worlds/Legends|Galactic Core]]. Furthermore, the initial phase of [[Operation Shadow Hand]] had begun, launching fleets of [[World Devastator]]s, brainchildren of [[Umak Leth]]: machines which destroyed everything in their path, plunging them into vast furnaces which produced weapons and items which could repair or build new World Devastators. The first target, selected for its long and steadfast support of the Rebellion, was the planet [[Dac/Legends|Mon Calamari]]. The recently rescued Lando and his crew were dispatched aboard the ''Emancipator'' to fight a holding action while the main Rebel fleets assembled. [[File:Subservient_Luke.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Luke Skywalker kneels before Palpatine.]] At this time, Luke Skywalker had reached [[Byss/Legends|Byss]], a Deep Core world enveloped in the power of the [[Dark side of the Force/Legends|dark side of the Force]]. There he met face-to-face with [[Palpatine/Legends|the Emperor]] reborn, who revealed that for many years, he took to [[Transfer essence|transferring]] his mind and consciousness to [[Cloning/Legends|clone]] bodies to attain [[Immortality/Legends|immortality]]. However, the clones were one crucial step from long life; they were more ravaged by the dark side than sustained by the Force. But Palpatine had numerous clones to laugh in the face of death, allowing him to rule indefinitely. The Emperor offered him apprenticeship and the prospect of controlling the enormous fleet of Devastators, as well as the rest of the [[Shadow Hand Fleet|Imperial fleet]]. Faced with an impossible situation and with a looming threat of doom for his friends and allies, Luke acquiesced to becoming his apprentice, hoping he could defeat the Emperor from the inside. At this time, R2-D2 was modified to carry the Master Signal for the World Devastators. {{Quote|Yes. My father's destiny is my own.|Luke Skywalker|}} Meanwhile, Lando's attack force began their [[Battle of Calamari|attack]] on the Imperial fleet. Though they were able to break through the Star Destroyer defenses, the Devastator ''[[Silencer-7]]'' turned the ''Emancipator'' to scrap and launched squadrons of robotic [[TIE series/Legends|TIEs]]. This forced the crew of the Republic Destroyer to abandon ship. At Pinnacle Base, Leia received a message from Luke, telling her not to follow him. Han Solo and Leia Organa Solo had gone to [[Nar Shaddaa/Legends|Nar Shaddaa]], to find transport to the Deep Core. After landing the ''Falcon'' in [[Shug Ninx]]'s garage, meeting Han's former girlfriend, [[Salla Zend]], Han and Leia went to Han's old living quarters. Along the way, they encountered a woman named [[Vima-Da-Boda]], who gave Leia a [[Lightsaber/Legends|lightsaber]]. Upon arriving at Han's place, they discovered that [[Boba Fett/Legends|Boba Fett]] was alive and that their friend, [[Mako Spince]], betrayed them. Pursued by the hunters, Han and Leia made it to the ''[[Starlight Intruder]]'', the ship that brought them to the Deep Core. Meanwhile, the Rebels in combat over the planet Mon Calamari received aid from the Alliance reserves, including the new [[E-7 E-wing multi-role starfighter|E-wings]]. Suddenly, three Devastators fell into the sea because Luke Skywalker shut them down from Byss. [[File:Leia_and_Sidious.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Palpatine attempting to turn Leia]] After arriving on Byss and missing Fett by a long shot, Leia piloted the ''Falcon'' to the [[Emperor's Citadel|Emperor's citadel]]. After she, C-3PO, Han and Chewbacca got off, Salla and Shug took the ''Falcon'' to a safe place. The four were brought before the Emperor and Luke. Despite trying to fight back, Leia was rendered unconscious and was brought to Palpatine's quarters to be turned. There, she discovered that he held hostage an even greater treasure: a mysterious and intelligent artifact called the [[Jedi holocron|Jedi Holocron]], which the Emperor had stolen during the [[Great Jedi Purge/Legends|Great Jedi Purge]] from [[Ashka Boda]]. With both the Holocron and Luke Skywalker under his control, the Emperor appeared to reign supreme in the Force. He revealed that he planned to overshadow Leia's third child stirring in her womb as his next new body. In anger, Leia toppled Palpatine from his bed and stole the Holocron. She found Luke, who claimed he was [[Light side of the Force/Legends|free of the dark side]], despite his eyes continuing to shine yellow. Meanwhile, Shug and Salla gained control of a [[Hunter-Killer probot|Hunter Killer patrol droid]] to blast Han and Chewie free. After being joined by Leia, Luke and the droids, the ''Falcon'' took off from Byss. However, Luke revealed that he had cast an illusory projection of himself to make them believe he had boarded so they would escape. [[File:LvS_clonelab.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Luke Skywalker attempts to subdue Palpatine.]] The real Luke Skywalker then marched into the clone lab during Palpatine's last few moments before the transition. When he revealed his purpose, Palpatine allowed his current incarnation to die, hoping to reach one of the clones before Luke destroyed them all. Skywalker went on a destructive spree through the lab, hoping to beat Palpatine to the clones. But he missed one. The newly resurrected Palpatine scoffed at Luke's attempts to vanquish him. When Luke attempted to subdue Palpatine, the [[Sith Lord]] seized one of the lightsabers he kept stored in the lab and [[Duel on Byss|engaged]] Luke. Despite his valiant efforts, Luke was disarmed and held at blade-point. Palpatine completed Skywalker's turn to the dark side, reducing him to an obedient watchdog. Meanwhile, on Mon Cala, the Devastators were still invincible. However, Luke installed a code into R2-D2's memory banks to shut down the Devastators. Though the great war machines were down, the crew managed to power up the factories until control was restored, but R2-D2 created a new code to make the Devastators recycle each other. But this was only a small part of the Emperor's plan. Great new fleets of the Empire were spreading across the Galactic Core, and soon [[The galaxy/Legends|the entire galaxy]] would fall under the sway of the dark side. [[File:Eclipse_Allegiance.jpg|thumb|left|200px|''Eclipse'' arrives at Pinnacle Base.]] Luke traveled with the Emperor to capture his sister, aboard the recently completed and christened flagship, the [[Eclipse (Eclipse-class)|''Eclipse'']]. Prior to the ''Eclipse''{{'s}} arrival, the Holocron which Leia had stolen from the Emperor delivered a thousand-year-old prophecy to the Princess, which told of her battle against the young Emperor: {{Quote|A brother and sister
born to walk the sky,
but reckless brother falls—
into dark side's eye!
Jedi sister carries ''hope''
for future in her womb.
Only ''she'' can save the
Skywalkers from
certain doom!
A Jedi-killer
wants to tame her.
Now the dark-side lord
comes to claim her.
She must battle, join
against this thief,
or the dynasty of ''all'' the
Jedi will come to grief!|[[Bodo Baas]]|[[Dark Empire 6|''Dark Empire'' 6]]}} [[File:L_versus_s.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Skywalker vs. Palpatine]] As the ''Eclipse'' came out of hyperspace, the Emperor made his demands clear to [[Alliance High Command/Legends|Alliance Command]] for Leia to come to his flagship alone, with the [[Holocron/Legends|holocron]]; he even promised to discuss a truce. Despite Han's objections, Leia boarded the shuttle, which took her to the Emperor's ''Eclipse''-class [[Super Star Destroyer/Legends|Super Star Destroyer]]. It was she who appealed to the good inside Luke, redeeming him. Outraged, the Emperor fought Luke in a [[Lightsaber combat/Legends|lightsaber battle]], ultimately losing his hand. However, an enraged Palpatine unleashed a titanic [[Force storm (wormhole)|force storm]] to obliterate the Rebel Alliance fleet. Using knowledge taught to them, brother and sister turned the Force storm upon its dark master. The result was the destruction of the ''Eclipse'' and Palpatine. As the ''Eclipse'' was destroyed, the twins fled on a shuttle. Once safely aboard an Alliance cruiser, Luke, his eyes once again blue, proclaimed: {{Quote|The Jedi Knights ''will'' rise again.|Luke Skywalker|}} ==Development== On [[November 19]], [[1988]], [[Tom Veitch]] sent a short letter to [[George Lucas]] proposing a new ''[[Star Wars]]'' [[Star Wars comic books|comic book]] series, to be written by Veitch and illustrated by [[Cam Kennedy]]. At that time, no new ''Star Wars'' comic books or [[List of books|novels]] were being published, but Veitch, a long-time fan of the series, believed the franchise to still have further narrative potential outside the film medium. Veitch included samples from his work with Kennedy on [[Wikipedia:The Light and Darkness War|''The Light and Darkness War'']] series in his proposal, and, although he knew the chances were slim, awaited a reply. Three days later, on [[November 22]], [[Lucy Autrey Wilson]] from [[Lucas Licensing]] called Veitch back, expressing [[Lucasfilm]]'s interest in the proposal, pending Lucas' approval of the concept after seeing the creative team's samples. Lucas, a long-time comic book fan, approved the proposal, and on [[November 29]], Wilson called Veitch to inform him the project was a go.{{InsiderCite|157|Of Rebel Dreams and Dark Empires: The Making of a Star Wars Legend|Of Rebel Dreams and Dark Empires: The Making of a ''Star Wars'' Legend}} Before the writing process could begin, however, a few important issues had to be addressed -- namely, the familiarity of the artists with the franchise, and what the story would be about. Regarding the first point, Veitch was very familiar with the films, but Kennedy had actually never seen the ''Star Wars'' films before. Before beginning work on it, Kennedy watched the films on video cassette, and felt inspired to begin the series. Kennedy then moved from [[Wikipedia:Scotland|Scotland]] to [[Wikipedia:Oregon|Oregon]] for two years to begin work with Veitch.{{WebCite|url=https://www.listennotes.com/podcasts/comic-scene-the/comic-scene-the-podcast-NrthFE9s6IR|text=Comic Scene The Podcast Episode 1|date=2019-02-05|work=[https://www.listennotes.com Listen Notes]|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/gkDHs}} When it came to the story concept, it went through several iterations before becoming the comic it became. The first proposal, which Veitch made on December 2, was entitled ''The Jedi Chronicles'' and would have been a prequel to the ''Star Wars'' [[original trilogy]] films.{{InsiderCite|158|Of Light and Darkness: The Making of Dark Empire – Part II|Of Light and Darkness: The Making of ''Dark Empire'' – Part II}} Within this proposal, Veitch described the premise as such: ''"Our first ambition is to examine the [[Rise of the Empire era|period]] between the first and second trilogies, when [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]] rose to preeminence as '[[Dark Lord of the Sith/Legends|Dark Lord of the Sith]].' We are told that it was during this 20 year span following the [[Clone Wars/Legends|Clone Wars]] that Vader, '[[Great Jedi Purge/Legends|hunted down and exterminated]] the remaining [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]]' -- all except [[Yoda/Legends|Yoda]] and [[Obi-Wan Kenobi/Legends|Obi-Wan Kenobi]]. We would want to focus on the greatness of the martyred [[Jedi Master/Legends|Masters]], their wise and ingenious use of [[The Force/Legends|the Force]], their awesome exploits against the [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Empire]], and ultimately their heroic [[Death/Legends|deaths]]. Our book would end on an upbeat note, with Obi-Wan Kenobi watching young [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] from afar, prophesying Luke's role in the struggle and victories yet to come."'' Lucy Autrey Wilson, Director of Publishing and the Lucasfilm editor who worked with Veitch on the series, rejected this proposal on the grounds that it might interfere with the forthcoming ''Star Wars'' [[prequel trilogy]] films. She suggested instead that Veitch consider focusing the comic line on the [[Post-Imperial era/Legends|time period]] after the [[1983]] film {{Film|VI}}. Veitch and Kennedy subsequently agreed to Wilson's proposed timeline for the series. Veitch was excited by the prospect of continuing the story after ''Return of the Jedi'', but felt daunted by the task. Still, Veitch was taken in by the opportunities the era provided, later saying: ''"I leaped at the opportunity to explore the mythological underpinnings of the Force -- both the [[Light side of the Force/Legends|light]] and the [[Dark side of the Force/Legends|dark side]]."''{{InsiderCite|159|Death to the Dark Side!: The Making of Dark Empire – Part III|Death to the Dark Side!: The Making of ''Dark Empire'' – Part III}} Drawing on inspiration from ''The Light and Darkness War'', Veitch sent in a new proposal suggesting the return of [[Galactic Emperor/Legends|Emperor]] [[Palpatine/Legends|Palpatine]] to [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]]. In this proposal, Veitch suggested several possible explanations for Palpatine's return, such as the notion that Palpatine was a [[Cyborg/Legends|machine]], along with another suggestion that Palpatine in the films was actually possessed by an extra-galactic "[[Parasite/Legends|parasitical]] intelligence." The most lasting suggestion was the idea that Palpatine had been [[Cloning/Legends|cloned]], and through [[Transfer essence|essence transfer]] had transferred his consciousness from one body to another; his goal of the series would be to eventually do the same with Luke Skywalker's body. It was this proposal that Wilson approved, but the proposed outline would undergo ongoing revisions. While some people showed opposition over seeing Palpatine back, Veitch saw no problem in this, citing the influence of the [[Wikipedia:Flash Gordon|Flash Gordon's]] franchise in ''Star Wars'', specifically how the title character's great archenemy [[Wikipedia:Ming the Merciless|Ming the Merciless]] never being completely killed off, such as in [[Wikipedia:Mike Hodges|Mike Hodges']] [[1980]] [[Wikipedia:Flash Gordon (film)|film adaptation]]. In regards to the characters maturing since ''Return of the Jedi'', Veitch opted to have Luke now a fully realized Jedi and Han and Leia's relationship not having anymore the teenage-like feuds from the films, having little time to enjoy themselves and each of their short moments being passionate and intense.{{WebCite|url=https://starwarsinterviews.com/reviews/exclusive-16-pages-of-dark-empire-author-tom-veitchs-new-star-wars-book|text=Exclusive: 16 pages of Dark Empire author Tom Veitch's new Star Wars book!|author=[[Dennis Pellegrom]]|work=[[Star Wars Interviews]]|archivedate=20201107235055|nolive=1}} Throughout this process, Veitch was in communication with [[Archie Goodwin]] with [[Marvel Comics|Marvel Epic]] about the series. On [[December 30]], 1988, Veitch sent his nine-page proposal to Goodwin, and Goodwin gave his feedback and suggested some ideas of his own. One such idea was the return of [[Darth Vader's armor/Legends|Vader's costume]]; as Veitch would explain, ''"Archie came up with the idea that the Empire would put somebody else inside Vader's costume, to inspire fear throughout the [[Galaxy/Legends|galaxy]]."'' Veitch revised his proposal based on Goodwin's feedback and suggestions, and he then sent in the new proposal to Lucasfilm. Wilson approved Veitch to begin writing scripts based on this outline, but she rejected any use of Vader's [[Armor/Legends|costume]] for the comic book series on the grounds that it could interfere with Lucas' usage of Vader or his likeness for the prequel films. Wilson also approved the usage of the film actors' likenesses as visual inspiration for the drawing of the characters' faces. Though work on the project had begun, and plans were in place for it to be published with Marvel, Goodwin left the comic book company in [[May]] of [[1989]], and the new Marvel editor did not share Goodwin's enthusiasm for the project, leaving the series in jeopardy. In spite of this, due to a year of discussion, negotiation and story treatments, Veitch submitted a 50 pages synopsis to Lucasfilm on [[September 25]], 1989 for a 144 pages comic book to be drawn/painted by Kennedy and published as a three 48 pages comic books. At that moment, Veitch had no idea how the story would end other than Luke and Leia would overpower Palpatine by joining their minds with the Force. The following month in [[October]], Lucasfilm approached Veitch on if he would be interested to do a [[Star Wars: Dark Empire (novelization)|novel adaptation]] of ''Dark Empire'' for [[Bantam Spectra]], with whom they were negotiating to make a new ''Star Wars'' novel. However, while interested on the idea, Bantam preferred to hire one of their contract writers, resulting in [[Timothy Zahn]]'s hiring on [[November 6]], 1989 due to write what became the [[1991]] novel ''[[Heir to the Empire]]'', as Zahn was becoming famous in the world of science-fiction. Ignoring a disappointed Veitch's offer to coordinate ideas and plotlines with Zahn, Bantam kept Zahn in the dark about Veitch's project and someone there suggested to make a [[Star Wars: Heir to the Empire (comic series)|comic book adaptation]] of Zahn's novel in light of the success of [[Wikipedia:Grant Morrison|Grant Morrison]]'s 1989 [[Wikipedia:Batman|Batman]] graphic novel [[Wikipedia:Arkham Asylum: A Serious House on Serious Earth|''Arkham Asylum: A Serious House on Serious Earth'']] in direct market sales, but Lucasfilm honored their agreement with Veitch and kept negotiating contracts with Marvel. Once Bantam made Zahn aware of ''Dark Empire'', Veitch was asked to critique ''Heir to the Empire'' and Zahn was asked to do the same with ''Dark Empire'', with Zahn disliking how ''Dark Empire'' brought Palpatine back (which Veitch attributed as Zahn missing some of Episode VI's subtext in regards to Palpatine's intentions with Luke) while Veitch disliked how ''Heir to the Empire'' felt uncinematic.{{WebCite|url=https://www.starwarsunderworld.com/2016/08/interview-with-dark-empire-writer-tom.html|text=Interview with ''Dark Empire'' Writer Tom Veitch|work=Star Wars Underworld|archivedate=20200613234534}} Both Veitch and Goodwin found Zahn's story pedestrian and unexciting due to seemingly rehasing the movies and making "knock-offs" of film characters for new characters, while deeming their own idea to bring Palpatine back better due to building on the tremendous power Palpatine, [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]] and [[Boba Fett/Legends|Boba Fett]] already held over the viewer's imagination, with Palpatine being the very essence of the dark side still thriving and more powerful than ever. A few more of Zahn's criticisms were the [[Force storm (wormhole)|Force storms]], how a [[Star Destroyer/Legends|Star Destroyer]] could crashland, that large-sized ships should stay away from [[Planet/Legends|planet]]-sized bodies, Leia not having [[Leia's Lightsaber|her own lightsaber]] when the story starts and using [[Telekinesis/Legends|telekinesis]] to blow up droids, Veitch's own statement on Luke having all the power of past Jedi, how [[Da Soocha V]] was geologically possible, the cooperating relationships of [[Dark Jedi/Legends|Dark Jedi]] on [[Byss/Legends|Byss]] and the way Luke and Leia would use mental power to defeat the Emperor (which he found completely, absolutely and insultingly wrong), for which all Veitch had in-universe rationalizations. Veitch felt that Han and Leia's union meant a child was inevitable, but Zahn did the same through [[Darth Caedus|Jacen]] and [[Jaina Solo Fel|Jaina Solo]], prompting Veitch to create [[Anakin Solo]]. By spring of [[1990]], the art for the first issue was completed, but contracts were still not finalized with Marvel for the series, resulting in Kennedy not being paid for his work so far. Though a print ad had been released for a three-issue iteration of the series with Marvel, planned for release in fall of 1990,{{WebCite|url=https://www.cbr.com/comic-book-urban-legends-revealed-131|text=Comic Book Urban Legends Revealed #131|author=Brian Cronin|date=2007-29-11|work=[[Wikipedia:Comic Book Resources|''Comic Book Resources'']]|archivedate=20200115165724}} Kennedy was considering leaving the project for other work, due to lack of pay and progress with the series. After more delays over the following months, Kennedy informed Veitch and Wilson that he would have to leave the project to seek other work. Veitch was distraught at the news, but, determined to save the project, suggested other artists to complete the series, such as [[Wikipedia:Andy Kubert|Andy]] and [[Wikipedia:Adam Kubert|Adam Kubert]]. Wilson was a big fan of Kennedy's art style, referring to it as 'brilliant,' and refused to let further problems with the series lead Kennedy to leave it, leading her to explore other options to expedite its publication.{{WebCite|url=https://talkingbay94.libsyn.com/lucy-autrey-wilson-director-of-the-expanded-universe-and-first-employee-of-lucasfilm|text=Lucy Autrey Wilson: Director of the Expanded Universe and First Employee of Lucasfilm|date=2022-03-30|work=[https://talkingbay94.libsyn.com Talking Bay 94]|archivedate=20220812225003}} In a fortuitous turn of events, [[Mike Richardson]], owner of [[Dark Horse Comics]], had seen some of Kennedy's artwork for the first issue of ''Dark Empire'' and was very interested in taking on the project. Richardson, who was friends with Kennedy, believed he could bring Kennedy back into the project if ''Dark Empire'' was published with Dark Horse. In [[August]] of 1990, at the [[Wikipedia:San Diego Comic-Con|San Diego Comic-Con]], Richardson and [[Randy Stradley]] met with Wilson to discuss publishing ''Dark Empire'' at Dark Horse, and they also proposed the idea of Dark Horse acquiring the ''Star Wars'' license for comic books. Although Marvel would lobby to publish the comic book, Wilson chose to pursue the transfer of the Lucasfilm comic book license from Marvel to Dark Horse. ''Dark Empire'' had found a new home, and with it, a new avenue for publication; in [[December]] of 1990, Veitch wrote to Kennedy to bring him back on board the project. As work restarted on the project, new continuity issues arose with the new production of ''Heir to the Empire'', now the first in a line of new ''Star Wars'' novels. The Lucasfilm editorial staff realized that if the two projects were not in proper continuity with each other, it could hurt sales; with this in mind, they had asked Zahn and Veitch to review each other's outlines, but both authors had very different visions of the universe and they could not agree on a unified vision. After Veitch initially proposed ''Dark Empire'' take place [[5 ABY/Legends|one year after]] ''Return of the Jedi'', Zahn was not inclined to reference the events of that series, possibly leading to a conundrum in the timeline;{{Fact}} Veitch proposed to Lucasfilm a simple resolution: he would set ''Dark Empire''{{'s}} events [[10 ABY/Legends|one year after]] Zahn's books and write an [[Opening crawl|opening scroll]] to bridge the two projects. Lucasfilm agreed, and the timeline was set. Work continued on the series, and the [[Dark Empire 1|first issue]] of ''Dark Empire'' was published on [[December 12]], 1991, almost exactly three years after the first proposal for the series was made. In 1992, Veitch explained, ''“Lucy Wilson said to us, you can't bring back Vader, but you can bring back the Emperor... if you can figure out how to do it.”''{{LucasFanClubCite|16|Star Wars Returns to Comics: Fan Club Exclusive}} In subsequent years, Veitch claimed in different interviews that George Lucas himself was involved in the development of the project.{{WebCite|url=http://www.jazmaonline.com/interviews/interviews2007.asp?intID=432|text=Interview with Tom Veitch|author=Richard Vasseur|date=2007-04-02|work=[http://www.jazmaonline.com Jazma Online]|archivedate=20070213005025}} ''[[Star Wars Insider]]''{{'s}} series of retrospectives on the creation of the series in [[2015]] clarified that Veitch's editorial contact throughout the development of the series was Wilson. This was further illuminated in [[2022]], when Wilson spoke about the development of the series, saying: ''"Tom Veitch, the story he wrote... You know, we were kind of letting people do what they wanted then. He wrote his story. I didn't ask George anything."'' ==Release and reception== [[File:DarkEmpirepromo.jpg|thumb|left|200px|1990 promotional art by Cam Kennedy]] The release of ''Dark Empire'' was enormously commercially successful, and effectively restarted the ''Star Wars'' comic book publishing program. Author Tom Veitch recalled in [[2003]] that he received letters of people saying ''Dark Empire'' was the best of the post-''Return of the Jedi'' stories and that it should be made into a movie or novel.{{WebCite|url=http://www.ugo.com/channels/comics/features/starwars|text=''Star Wars'' Comics Group Interview|author=Alex Ness|date=2003-12-03|work=[[Wikipedia:UGO Networks|UGO]]|archivedate=20040603120517}} As artist Cam Kennedy recounted, ''"Previously, Marvel had been selling about 4 to 5 thousand copies a month, and they had to sell about 7 and a half to cover costs. So it wasn't going anywhere. The first Star Wars I think we did, we did about 220,000. Which was good, and obviously Lucasfilm was very delighted. It kept selling and selling."'' [[Michael Kogge]], writing in a series of retrospectives on the series for ''Star Wars Insider'' in 2015, wrote that the success of ''Dark Empire'' "stunned the comics world" and "changed how licensed properties were handled in the industry." Many in the industry were enthusiastic in their praise for the comics' painted artwork. Dark Horse editor [[Barbara Kesel]] commented, ''"Cam's painted art was a striking new approach for comics, let alone 'licensed' comics. The intense colors and watercolor approach were so visually different from the average comics of the time that the series would have stood out on the stands even without a good story."'' Dark Horse owner Mike Richardson would proclaim, ''"Cam Kennedy is the best Star Wars artist in comics. Nobody else even comes close."'' Kennedy would later tell the story about how, at a New York convention, the famed French artist [[Wikipedia:Jean Giraud|Moebius]] exchanged some of his own art for that of ''Dark Empire.'' Legendary ''Star Wars'' concept artist [[Ralph McQuarrie]] would also praise the artwork of the series, going on to write the introduction for ''[[Star Wars: Dark Empire II|Dark Empire II]].'' In it, McQuarrie enthused about the original run of ''Dark Empire'', writing: ''"I haven't been an avid collector of comics and science fiction or fantasy art, but I've seen quite a lot, and I'll say the work in the Dark Empire series is very impressive. I look with envy at the crisp, stylized drawings by Cam Kennedy, the rich color and painterly effects [[Dave Dorman]] gets into his work."'' McQuarrie would even go further in his praise, adding: ''"I'm certain Cam Kennedy or any number of people could have read the script I read and designed the things as well or better."''''[[Star Wars: Dark Empire II]]'' George Lucas had a multifaceted response to the comic. Lucas responded favorably to the artwork of the comic; he bought 14 of the 16 art pieces that Dorman created for the series.{{WebCite|url=https://www.wired.com/1999/03/the-dark-force-of-licensing|text=The Dark Force of Licensing|author=Heidi Kriz|date=1999-03-12|work=[[Wikipedia:Wired (magazine)|''Wired'']]|archivedate=20211121004911}} A long-time comics enthusiast, Lucas' enjoyment of the art of ''Dark Empire'' led to its release to Lucasfilm employees as part of the annual Christmas gift, as would also be done for ''[[Star Wars: Tales of the Jedi (comic series)|Tales of the Jedi]]''. Lucas would eventually also choose to include cover art by Dorman from the series in his collection of favorite ''Star Wars'' comic art pieces as part of the ''[[Star Wars Art: Comics]]'' book.''[[Star Wars Art: Comics]]'' By contrast, Lucas would be more critical of the final story of ''Dark Empire''; while Veitch suggested publicly in interviews a positive reaction, Publishing Director Lucy Autrey Wilson revealed in 2022, ''"Later, George would run into me and he would say, "You know, Lucy, I would never clone the Emperor. He doesn't get cloned!" And I'm like, "How am I supposed to know that if you don't want to be involved in what we're doing? And yeah, if I do something you don't like?" So I started then having to send him memos. You know, this person wants to do this, this, and this; is this okay, is that okay.''" It was the publication of ''Dark Empire'' that prompted Lucas afterwards to take a more participatory role in the creation of the following stories in the Expanded Universe, with more oversight from him in the form of reviewed memos and outlines.{{WebCite|url=https://starwarsinterviews.com/star-wars-crew/lucy-autrey-wilson-lucasfilm-director-of-publishing|text=Lucy Autrey Wilson (Lucasfilm Director of Publishing)|author=[[Dennis Pellegrom]]|date=2022-08|work=[[Star Wars Interviews]]|archivedate=20201107235055|nolive=1}} In [[2008]], Lucas specifically mentioned Palpatine's cloning as something that stories following the events of ''Return of the Jedi'' did that didn't fit his vision following the films, emphasizing his preference for the conclusion of the general story arc with ''Return of the Jedi''.{{BookCite|title=''[[Total Film]]''|number=140|publisher=[[Wikipedia:Future plc|Future plc]]|date=2008-05}} In [[2019]] actor [[Ian McDiarmid]] would reaffirm that Lucas was steadfast in his refusal of Palpatine's return in any form, citing a conversation with Lucas where McDiarmid asked if Palpatine could return, and Lucas responded, ''"No, he's dead"''; McDiarmid replied with, ''"Well, wouldn’t it be interesting if...?"'' and Lucas promptly answered, ''"Forget it."''{{WebCite|url=https://www.polygon.com/star-wars-celebration/2019/4/15/18311680/star-wars-episode-ix-emperor-palpatine-dead-theory-rumors-rise-of-skywalker/|title=Ian McDiarmid says the Emperor is definitely ‘dead’ before Episode IX|author=Charlie Hall|date=2019-04-15|quote=Multiple times during the panel he returned to conversations he had had with George Lucas over the years about the fate of Palpatine. “‘Is he...?’ And before I could even finish the sentence,” McDiarmid recalled, imitating Lucas. “‘Dead, yes.’ ‘Well, couldn’t he perhaps...’ ‘No. He’s dead.’ ‘Well, wouldn’t it be interesting if...?’ ‘Forget it.’”|archivedate=20190504202107}} ''Dark Empire'''s notion of Palpatine's return was later revisited in the film ''[[Star Wars: Episode IX The Rise of Skywalker|Rise of Skywalker]]'', and when discussing Lucas' thoughts on the resurrection of Palpatine being brought to the silver screen, Wilson remarked, ''"He reacted by rolling his eyes... He was furious, I think."'' Some contributors to the [[Star Wars Legends|Expanded Universe]] made known publicly their dissatisfaction with the decision to bring Palpatine back to life, including Del Rey editor [[Shelly Shapiro]].{{InsiderCite|66|Novel Approach}} Shapiro would remark, ''"Frankly, I always felt that allowing the Emperor to come back — even via a clone — was a mistake. I felt it took away from the victory of the first three movies, minimized all Luke, Leia, and Han had fought for and won."'' In ''[[Vision of the Future]]'', Timothy Zahn included a conversation that refers to the events of ''Dark Empire'' with Luke Skywalker mentioning "the resurrected Emperor" and [[Mara Jade Skywalker|Mara Jade]] retorting, "Whatever. Personally, I’m not convinced it was really him."''[[Vision of the Future]]'' Zahn had not been a fan of the concept of Palpatine's return, and saw this as an opportunity to shed a different light on the comic, remarking, ''"Other authors' visions of what Star Wars is and what it should be are not necessarily what I would do. And one reason I agreed to do the [[Star Wars: The Hand of Thrawn Duology|Hand of Thrawn]] was to kind of get the last word, so to speak. I wanted one more bit of input into my vision of Star Wars. So I did what I thought was useful and necessary and made sense, and Lucasfilm apparently agreed."''{{WebCite|url=https://theforce.net/jedicouncil/interview/zahn.asp|title=Interviews - Face To Face With The Masters - Timothy Zahn|author=Chris Knight|date=2000-02|archivedate=20041212210859}} ==Legacy== Characters and events mentioned in the holocron used in ''Dark Empire'' formed the basis of the [[Star Wars: Tales of the Jedi (comic series)|''Tales of the Jedi'']] series, authored by Veitch and [[Kevin J. Anderson]], and several story elements of ''Dark Empire'' would be referenced in Anderson's ''[[Star Wars: The Jedi Academy Trilogy|Jedi Academy]]'' trilogy of novels.[[Star Wars: Tales of the Jedi (comic series)|''Star Wars: Tales of the Jedi'' comic series]][[Star Wars: The Jedi Academy Trilogy]] One of the events of the ''Dark Empire'' series, the [[Battle of Calamari]], was featured as a playable level in 1998's ''[[Star Wars: Rogue Squadron (video game)|Rogue Squadron]]'' game, where the player can fly a New Republic [[V-wing airspeeder|V-Wing]] against the [[World Devastator]]s, in defense of [[Dac/Legends|Mon Calamari]].[[Star Wars: Rogue Squadron (video game)|''Rogue Squadron'']] Additionally, the New Republic [[E-7 E-wing multi-role starfighter|E-Wing]] would become a staple of the Expanded Universe stories following ''Dark Empire'', featuring prominently in the ''[[The Black Fleet Crisis|Black Fleet Crisis]]'' and ''[[Star Wars: The New Jedi Order|New Jedi Order]]'' novels.[[The Black Fleet Crisis]][[Star Wars: The New Jedi Order]] ''[[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|Revenge of the Sith]]'' portrays Palpatine claiming to know the path of immortality, specifically [[Tragedy of Darth Plagueis the Wise/Legends|mentioning]] how his master [[Darth Plagueis/Legends|Darth Plagueis]] could [[Midi-chlorian manipulation|manipulate]] the [[Midi-chlorian/Legends|midi-chlorians]] to keep the ones he cared the most from dying.[[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith]] Veitch noticed this and liked how the film seemingly foreshadowed Palpatine's cloning scheme of ''Dark Empire'' in his pursuit for immortality. However, Lucas would make clear in interviews that this element of ''Revenge of the Sith'' was simply a manipulative lie by Palpatine, and that he had no such knowledge of any ability to cheat death, elaborating, ''"[Palpatine was] saying that 'My mentor told me that there was a way that you could stop death.' Which was a lie. They can't. Anakin got sold a bill of goods because he wanted it so bad that he'd believe anything anybody would sell him. It's a scam. Anakin's made a pact with the Devil."''[[The Star Wars Archives: Episodes I–III, 1999–2005]]{{Mediacat|from=1|imagecat=Images from Star Wars: Dark Empire}} ===Issues=== {{Comictable-colors|arc=no}} {{Comictable-start|Hardcover|[[Epic Collection]]|[[Marvel Omnibus]]}} {{Comictable-issue|1|[[Dark Empire 1|''The Destiny of a Jedi'']]|[[December 12]], [[1991]]|collected|tpb= {{Comictable-tpb|[[File:Darkempiretpb.jpg|100px]]|[[Star Wars: Dark Empire (trade paperback)|''Dark Empire'']]
